title: Session 8: Your AI Strategy
text:
The Course Journey



1


Session 1


What AI Can Do for You — personal productivity with AI




2


Session 2


Iterate, Critique, Trust — the maker/checker framework




3


Session 3


AI Meets Your Data — enterprise data access




4


Session 4


From Data to Deliverable — reporting pipelines and the agent loop




5

What Does Deployment Look Like? — architecture, data security, build vs. buy




6


Session 6


Can You Trust It? — validation, governance, guardrails




7


Session 7


Document AI — answers from your own files




8


Today


Your AI Strategy — capstone and synthesis




Welcome to our final session. Before we dive into your presentations, let me take thirty seconds to remind you where we have been. Look at this timeline. In Session 1, you discovered what AI can do for you personally — charts, documents, analyses in minutes. Session 2, you learned the maker/checker framework — iterate, critique, and calibrate your trust. Session 3, AI met your enterprise data — querying CRMs, ERPs, and HR systems in plain English. Session 4, you went from data to deliverable — reporting pipelines and the agent loop. Session 5, deployment — architecture, data security, build versus buy. Session 6, trust and verification — red-teaming, governance, guardrails. Session 7, document AI — retrieval-augmented generation for unstructured knowledge.
And today, Session 8, you bring it all together. Each of you has written a strategy document that draws on everything you have learned. Today you present it, get feedback, and leave with a concrete plan you can execute starting tomorrow. This is the session where the learning becomes action.
[TIMING: About 2 minutes on this slide. It is a fast recap, not a re-teaching. The timeline visual does the heavy lifting. Keep it brisk and forward-looking. You need to be into the setup by minute 3.]

title: Session 8: Your AI Strategy
text: Today’s Objective

title: Session 8: Your AI Strategy
section: Present a credible, scoped AI adoption strategy and leave with a concrete next step
text:
Present a credible, scoped AI adoption strategy and leave with a concrete next step

One objective today, and it is simple. Present a credible, scoped AI adoption strategy and leave with a concrete next step. Not a twelve-month roadmap. Not a committee to form. A specific action you will take, with a specific person, on a specific date. That is what “concrete” means in this context.
The emphasis is on “credible” and “scoped.” The best strategies you will hear today will not be the most ambitious. They will be the most actionable — tight enough to pilot in thirty days, specific enough that a CFO would fund them, grounded enough in what you have learned about governance and reliability that they will not blow up on contact with reality.
[TIMING: About 30 seconds. This is a signpost. Move quickly to the setup.]

title: Session 8: Your AI Strategy
text:
The Setup

Let me frame how today is going to work. We have a structured format that gives everyone a chance to present and ensures the strongest strategies get full-class exposure.
[TIMING: Brief pause, then advance.]

text: Your Audience

title: Session 8: Your AI Strategy
section: You are presenting to your executive team. Your breakout group is your board of advisors.
text:
You are presenting to your executive team. Your breakout group is your board of advisors.

Convince them this is worth funding.


Here is the framing I want you to hold in your mind for the next ninety minutes. You are not presenting to a professor. You are not completing an assignment. You are presenting to your executive team. Your breakout group is your board of advisors — experienced peers who will push back, ask hard questions, and help you sharpen your thinking. The standard is simple: convince them this is worth funding.
That means your pitch needs a clear problem, a specific solution, a credible business case, a governance plan, and a Day 1 action that is not “let’s schedule a meeting.” Treat this as the real thing, because for many of you, this strategy document could become the actual proposal you bring to your leadership.
[TIMING: About 1 minute. You should be at minute 3. Transition to the format slide.]

title: Session 8: Your AI Strategy
text:
Capstone Format



1


Breakout Pitches


5 groups of 6. Each person gives a 2-minute pitch + 1 min feedback. Group votes on the strongest.




2


Plenary Presentations


5 winning strategies presented to the full class. 5 min each + 3 min Q&A from peers.




3


Instructor Feedback


Feedback on pilot scope, business case, governance, and Day 1 action for each strategy.




Here is the format. Three steps. Step 1: breakout pitches. I am putting you into five groups of six. Each person gives a two-minute pitch followed by one minute of feedback from the group. After everyone has presented, the group votes on the strongest strategy to represent them in the plenary. That is about twenty-seven minutes of breakout time total.
Step 2: plenary presentations. The five winning strategies come back to the full class. Each presenter gets five minutes to present, followed by three minutes of Q&A and feedback from peers. That is forty minutes for the plenary round.
Step 3: instructor feedback. After each plenary presentation, I will give brief feedback on four dimensions — is the pilot scoped tightly enough, is the business case credible, is the governance plan adequate, and does the Day 1 action actually happen on Day 1.
Everyone presents in the breakout. Five advance to the plenary. Even if your strategy is not selected for the plenary, the breakout feedback is valuable — and you may hear ideas from other presenters that strengthen your own plan.
[TIMING: About 1.5 minutes on this slide. Make sure the format is clear before anyone goes into breakout rooms. You should be approaching the evaluation criteria slide.]

title: Session 8: Your AI Strategy
text:
What Makes a Strong Pitch


Evaluation Dimensions


Specificity — a concrete use case, not “we’ll use AI for analytics”
Business case — who benefits, what decision gets faster, what’s the dollar impact
Data readiness — where does the data live, what’s the integration challenge
Governance — who validates answers, what are the guardrails
Day 1 action — what happens Monday morning, not “we’ll form a committee”



The best pitches are narrow and actionable. “We’ll deploy document AI on our 200 vendor contracts so procurement can answer compliance questions in seconds” beats “We’ll implement an enterprise AI strategy.”


Before you go into breakout rooms, let me remind you of the evaluation dimensions. These are from the rubric you used to self-check your strategy documents.
Specificity: a concrete use case with a named team, a named data source, and a named decision that gets better. Not “we will use AI for analytics.” That is a slogan, not a strategy.
Business case: who benefits, what decision gets faster, and ideally, what is the dollar impact? Even a rough estimate — “this saves our analysts twenty hours a week” — is better than nothing.
Data readiness: where does the data live, and what is the integration challenge? If you are proposing to query data across three systems with different naming conventions, say so. If you are proposing document AI on contracts that are scanned PDFs, acknowledge the ingestion challenge.
Governance: who validates the AI’s answers? What are the guardrails? We spent all of Session 6 on this. If your strategy does not address governance, it is not ready for production.
Day 1 action: what happens Monday morning? A specific person does a specific thing. Not “we will form a committee” or “we will explore options.” The best Day 1 actions I have seen are things like “I will email our VP of Sales with a one-page proposal and a request for thirty minutes” or “I will pull the top twenty vendor contracts and test them against Microsoft Copilot.”
Read the explainer at the bottom. “We will deploy document AI on our 200 vendor contracts so procurement can answer compliance questions in seconds” — that is narrow and actionable. “We will implement an enterprise AI strategy” — that is neither.
[TIMING: About 2 minutes on this slide. You should be at about minute 7. Time to launch breakout rooms.]
Transition: Alright, you know the format, you know the criteria. Let’s go. I am opening breakout rooms now.

title: Session 8: Your AI Strategy
text:
Breakout Round

[BREAKOUT INSTRUCTIONS: Open 5 breakout rooms in Zoom with 6 students each. Set a timer for 27 minutes. Each person gets 2 minutes to pitch plus 1 minute of feedback — that is 3 minutes per person, 18 minutes for all 6 presenters, plus about 5 minutes for the group to discuss and vote, plus a few minutes of buffer.
Visit each room briefly during the breakout. Listen for: - Strategies that are too broad (“transform our data culture”) — gently prompt them to narrow - Strategies that skip governance — remind them of Session 6 - Strategies with vague Day 1 actions — push for specificity - Strategies that combine database AI and document AI — these tend to be the strongest
When you visit a room, do not take over. Listen for 2-3 minutes, offer one brief observation or question, then move on. Your goal is to hear a sample of the pitches so you are prepared for the plenary.
At the 25-minute mark, broadcast a message: “Two minutes remaining. Please finalize your votes.”
At 27 minutes, close the breakout rooms.]
Alright, you are now in your breakout rooms. Each person gets two minutes for their pitch, followed by one minute of feedback. I will be visiting rooms to listen in. After everyone has presented, discuss as a group and vote on the strongest strategy to advance to the plenary. You have twenty-seven minutes. Go.
[TIMING: Breakout runs from minute 3 to minute 30. Visit 3-4 rooms during this time. Take notes on what you hear — you will reference these during instructor feedback in the plenary. At minute 28, broadcast the two-minute warning. Close rooms at minute 30.]
Welcome back everyone. Let me hear from each group. Which strategy was selected to represent your group, and who is presenting? Group 1, you are up first.

title: Session 8: Your AI Strategy
text:
Breakout Instructions



Your Pitch (2 minutes)


What’s the use case?
What data or documents does it use?
Who are the users and what decision does it improve?
What’s your Day 1 action?




Your Feedback (1 minute)


Is the scope tight enough to pilot in 30 days?
What’s the biggest risk?

This slide stays up during the breakout so people can reference it. Let me walk through both cards quickly before I open the rooms.
On the left, your pitch. You have two minutes. Hit four things: the use case, the data or documents it uses, who the users are and what decision it improves, and your Day 1 action. Two minutes is short, so do not try to cover all eight sections of your strategy document. Focus on the hook — why should we fund this — and the specifics that make it credible.
On the right, your feedback role. When someone else presents, you have one minute to react. Four prompts to guide you. Is the scope tight enough to pilot in thirty days? What is the biggest risk? What question would the CFO ask? And after everyone has presented, vote on which pitch should represent the group in the plenary.
For the feedback, I want you to be constructive but direct. This is a board of advisors, not a cheerleading squad. If the scope is too broad, say so. If the governance plan is missing, flag it. If the Day 1 action is vague, push for specificity. You are helping each other get to a stronger strategy.
[TIMING: This slide is shown alongside or just before the breakout opens. Spend about 1 minute talking through it, then open the rooms. This slide should remain visible in the breakout rooms for reference if possible.]

title: Session 8: Your AI Strategy
text:
Plenary Presentations

[PLENARY INSTRUCTIONS: This section runs from minute 30 to minute 70 — forty minutes for 5 presentations. Each presentation gets 5 minutes plus 3 minutes of Q&A and instructor feedback, totaling 8 minutes per presentation.
Format for each presentation: 1. Presenter shares screen (or presents verbally) — 5 minutes 2. Open the floor for 1-2 peer questions — about 1.5 minutes 3. Instructor feedback on the four dimensions — about 1.5 minutes 4. Transition to next presenter
Keep time strictly. At 4 minutes, give a one-minute warning. At 5 minutes, stop the presentation and open Q&A. At 8 minutes total, transition to the next presenter.
Instructor feedback should be brief and structured. For each presentation, comment on: - Pilot scope: Is it tight enough? One use case, one team, 30 days? - Business case: Is the value proposition credible? Would a CFO fund this? - Governance: Is there a verification plan? Who checks the AI’s answers? - Day 1 action: Is it specific? Person, task, date?
Be encouraging but honest. If a strategy is too broad, say so with a specific suggestion for how to narrow it. If governance is missing, flag it. If the Day 1 action is strong, celebrate it — others will learn from the example.]
Alright, we have five strategies advancing to the full class. Each presenter gets five minutes to present, followed by three minutes of Q&A and feedback from peers and from me. I will keep time strictly — at four minutes I will give you a one-minute warning, and at five minutes we open the floor.
Presenter 1, you are up. Go ahead.
[TIMING: 40 minutes total for the plenary, from minute 30 to minute 70. 8 minutes per presentation. Stay on time. If you fall behind, shorten your instructor feedback rather than cutting peer Q&A — the peer questions are often the most valuable part.
After all 5 presentations, take a brief moment to acknowledge all the presenters before transitioning to the synthesis. Something like: “Thank you to all five presenters, and to everyone who presented in the breakout rooms. Every one of you now has a strategy document you can take back to your organizations. Let me now pull together the themes from today and from the entire course.”]

title: Session 8: Your AI Strategy
text:
Synthesis: What You Now Know



Personal Productivity Multiplier

AI is available right now — charts, documents, analyses, automated workflows. The tools from Sessions 1–2 require no IT approval and no enterprise deployment.



Plain English = Data Answers

AI agents turn natural language questions into enterprise data answers — replacing dashboards, manual reports, and the wait for analyst time.



Simple but Not Easy

The technology is simpler than expected (50 lines of code) but production requires governance, verification, and organizational readiness.



Two Complementary Tools

Database AI for structured data (numbers, trends, KPIs). Document AI for unstructured text (policies, contracts, procedures). Most real questions need both.



The Critical Skill

Asking the right questions, iterating on output, and verifying the answers. The maker/checker mindset is the throughline of this entire course.



Let me pull together the five big ideas from this entire course. These are the things I want you to carry with you long after the details fade.
First, AI is a personal productivity multiplier. Charts, documents, analyses, automated workflows — all available right now, no IT approval needed. You experienced this in Sessions 1 and 2. The tools you used on day one are tools you can use tomorrow morning.
Second, plain English equals enterprise data answers. AI agents turn natural language questions into SQL queries, chart generation, and narrative summaries — replacing dashboards, manual reports, and the wait for analyst time. The person closest to the business question can now get the answer directly.
Third, simple but not easy. In Session 4, you saw that a working agent is about fifty lines of code. The technology is simpler than most people expect. But production deployment requires governance, verification, and organizational readiness. The gap between a demo and a production system is not technical — it is organizational. Who owns the data? Who validates the answers? Who approves deployment? Those are the hard questions, and several of the strategies we just heard addressed them well.
Fourth, two complementary tools. Database AI for structured data — numbers, trends, KPIs. Document AI for unstructured text — policies, contracts, procedures. You learned database AI in Sessions 3 through 6 and document AI in Session 7. Most real business questions need both, and the most powerful strategies we heard today combined both capabilities.
Fifth, the critical skill. The human skill that matters most is not building AI tools. It is asking the right questions, iterating on output, and verifying the answers. The maker/checker mindset is the throughline of this entire course. If you ask a bad question, you get a bad answer faster. If you do not verify, you propagate errors at scale. The maker/checker framework is not optional — it is essential.
[TIMING: About 5 minutes on this slide, from minute 70 to about minute 75. Walk through each card. Reference specific strategies from the plenary presentations when possible — “As [presenter name] showed, combining database AI with document AI for vendor management is exactly this third point in action.” Make it feel like a synthesis, not a new lecture.]
Transition: Those are the five big ideas. But before we close, I want to show you one more capability — something we have not covered yet, and it is the one that will change how you think about AI the most.

title: Session 8: Your AI Strategy
text:
Beyond Data

Everything we have done in this course has been about data. Querying it, analyzing it, reporting on it, retrieving it from documents. That is foundational, and it is where the immediate ROI lives. But there is one more capability I want to leave you with, and it goes beyond data retrieval into something more powerful.
[TIMING: Brief pause on the divider. Build a little anticipation. Then advance.]

title: Session 8: Your AI Strategy
text: AI as Thinking Partner

title: Session 8: Your AI Strategy
section: The real power isn’t asking what happened. It’s asking what should we do — and pushing back on the answer.
text:
The real power isn’t asking what happened. It’s asking what should we do — and pushing back on the answer.

AI anchors on whatever framing you provide. The human role is challenging assumptions, not outsourcing judgment.


Here is the shift. Everything we have done so far has been asking “what happened” — what were sales last quarter, what does the contract say, what did the board approve. Those are retrieval questions. The AI goes and gets information. But the real power is asking “what should we do” — and then pushing back on the answer.
When you ask a strategic question, the AI does not just retrieve data. It synthesizes across multiple sources, identifies patterns, and offers recommendations. That is powerful. But here is the critical caveat, and I want you to remember this. AI anchors on whatever framing you provide. If you ask “should we expand into the Southeast?” the AI will tend to find evidence that supports expansion — because that is how you framed the question. The human role is challenging assumptions, not outsourcing judgment. You push back. You reframe. You ask “what if our supply chain cannot handle the volume?” and see how the analysis changes.
This is where AI goes from being a reporting tool to being a thinking partner. But only if you treat it like a colleague you argue with, not an oracle you defer to.
[TIMING: About 2 minutes on this slide. Set up the demo that follows.]

title: Session 8: Your AI Strategy
text:
The Thinking Partner Demo



Data Question

“What were Q4 sales for the Energy Division?”

One answer. One number.
Useful, but limited.




Strategic Question

“I’m considering expanding the Energy Division. What are the top 3 risks based on our data?”

Synthesizes across sales, headcount, customer concentration, margin trends
Then: “Push back on risk #2 — what’s the evidence?”




The shift from “report pulling” to “strategic dialogue” is where the real ROI lives. But remember: AI anchors on your framing. Challenge its assumptions the way you’d challenge a consultant.


[DEMO INSTRUCTIONS: This is a live demo from minute 78 to about minute 86. Switch to the XYZ app. Have your fallback recording ready.
Demo sequence: 1. Ask the data question first: “What were Q4 sales for the Energy Division?” Show the simple answer — one number, one chart. Point out that this is what you have been doing all course. Useful, but limited.

Then ask the strategic question: “I’m considering expanding the Energy Division into the Southeast. Based on our pipeline, customer concentration, and support ticket trends, what are the top 3 risks?” Watch the agent reason across multiple systems — sales data, customer data, support tickets. It will synthesize and identify risks.
Then push back: “You’re assuming our supply chain can handle Southeast volume. What if it can’t?” Watch the agent adjust its analysis. This is the key moment — the AI revises its thinking based on your challenge.
If time permits, try one more push-back: “Push back on risk number 2. What’s the actual evidence for that?” Show that you can ask the AI to challenge its own reasoning.

The point is to model the behavior you want executives to adopt: treat AI as a colleague you argue with, not a tool you query.]
Let me show you the difference. I am going to ask two questions of the XYZ app. First, the data question on the left card: “What were Q4 sales for the Energy Division?” Watch — one number, one chart, done. That is useful, but it is just report pulling.
Now watch what happens when I ask the strategic question on the right card: “I am considering expanding the Energy Division into the Southeast. Based on our pipeline, customer concentration, and support ticket trends, what are the top 3 risks?” See how it reasons across multiple data sources? It is pulling sales trends, looking at customer concentration, checking support ticket patterns. It synthesizes and gives me three risks.
But here is where it gets interesting. I am going to push back. “You are assuming our supply chain can handle Southeast volume. What if it can’t?” Watch how the analysis changes. The AI adjusts, because I challenged one of its assumptions. This is the thinking partner in action. It does not get defensive. It revises. But — and this is crucial — it would never have challenged that assumption on its own. That is your job. AI anchors on your framing. The same data with different framing produces different recommendations. The human role is providing context, challenging assumptions, and deciding. Not outsourcing judgment.
[TIMING: About 8 minutes for this demo, from minute 78 to minute 86. Spend 1 minute on the data question, 3 minutes on the strategic question, 2 minutes on the push-back, and 2 minutes on discussion. Ask the group: “How many of you would have thought to challenge that assumption? That is the skill — knowing when to push back.”]
Transition: That is the vision. Data retrieval plus strategic dialogue. Now let me give you five things you can do starting tomorrow.

title: Session 8: Your AI Strategy
text:
Turn 1: The Strategic Question

“I’m considering expanding the Energy Division into the Southeast. Based on our pipeline, customer concentration, and support ticket trends, what are the top 3 risks?”

The agent queried three systems (Legacy CRM, NetSuite Finance, Zendesk) and returned:
Risk 1 — Thin pipeline: Only 14 deals totaling $2.1M in Southeast regions. Division generates $147M annually — the pipeline is 1.4% of that base. Average deal size 28% below division average.
Risk 2 — Customer concentration: Top 5 customers = 62% of Energy revenue. ExxonMobil alone = 23%. Expansion funded from division profits is vulnerable to a single account loss.
Risk 3 — Support strain: Tickets up 34% YoY, resolution time up from 18 to 27 hours. Adding a region increases load on an already degraded support team.

Turn 1 shows the agent synthesizing across three enterprise systems to frame a strategic question. Notice that the agent did not just pull one number — it queried CRM pipeline data, customer concentration figures, and Zendesk support trends, then wove them into a risk framework. Each risk is grounded in specific data points. This is what “thinking partner” looks like at its best: data-driven strategic framing in seconds.

title: Session 8: Your AI Strategy
text:
Turns 2–3: Pushback and Evidence

Turn 2: “You’re assuming our supply chain can handle Southeast volume. What if it can’t?”

The agent pulled Oracle SCM data it had not previously queried:

Houston warehouse at 91% capacity (pipes & fittings), lead times up 56% YoY
3 of top 10 suppliers are single-source (National Oilwell Varco at $4.5M/year)
Revised risk ranking: Supply chain promoted to #1, displacing thin pipeline


Turn 3: “Push back on risk #2. What’s the actual evidence?”

The agent separated its claims by confidence level:

High confidence: Delivery Issues tickets +60%, resolution time nearly doubled — this is real operational strain
Medium confidence: Ticket spike correlates with warehouse capacity data but causation is not proven
Lower confidence: Assumed shared support pool with Safety Division — no data confirming this


Turn 2: the agent updated its analysis when challenged — it is not anchored to its first answer. Turn 3: it decomposed its own confidence levels instead of defending its position.


These two turns are the critical moments. In Turn 2, the user challenged an unstated assumption — that supply chain could handle the expansion. The agent did not defend its prior analysis. It went and got new data from Oracle SCM, found the Houston warehouse at 91% capacity with lead times already degraded, and promoted supply chain to the number one risk. Most people treat AI as an oracle and accept the first answer. The quality of the output depends on the quality of the follow-up questions. In Turn 3, the user asked the agent to push back on its own risk assessment. Instead of defending its position, the agent separated its claims into high, medium, and lower confidence tiers, explicitly acknowledging where the evidence was thin. This is what distinguishes a thinking partner from a yes-machine.

title: Session 8: Your AI Strategy
text:
Turn 4: The Decision Boundary

“Given all of this, should we expand or not?”
Agent: “That’s not my decision to make — and you should be skeptical of any AI system that would make it for you.”




What Favors Expansion


Strategic diversification (62% top-5 concentration is a vulnerability)
Board alignment (Energy growth is a Horizon 1 priority)
Organic demand exists (14 deals without dedicated BD effort)




What Argues Against Expanding Now


Supply chain at 91% with 56% lead time increase
Support at tipping point (SLA breaches, 4-person team)
Pipeline is $2.1M against $8–10M breakeven threshold



The 3 unknowns that matter most: Can you invest in supply chain first? Is the 14-deal pipeline a leading indicator or a ceiling? What’s the cost of waiting 6 months?

The agent structured the decision without making it. It identified the unknowns the data can’t answer — which are the ones that matter most.


Turn 4 is the ethical boundary. The user asked for a recommendation, and the agent explicitly declined to make one. Instead, it structured the decision: what favors expansion, what argues against expanding now, and — most importantly — the three unknowns that should actually drive the decision. Can you invest in supply chain capacity before expanding? Is the thin pipeline a result of no BD effort or no market? What is the cost of waiting six months? These are questions the data cannot answer. The agent also proposed a sequenced approach: three months of dedicated BD effort plus supply chain planning, a gate review at month three, and regional buildout only if criteria are met. This is not an AI replacing human judgment. This is an AI making human judgment better-informed and better-structured. The key lesson: an AI that confidently says “yes, expand” based on 14 pipeline deals and strained supply chain data is more dangerous than one that says “here are the three things you need to figure out first.”

title: Session 8: Your AI Strategy
text:
Monday Morning Actions



A. Use AI for Your Personal Workflow

The tools from Sessions 1–2 are available now — no IT approval needed. Start with charts, documents, and analyses for your own work.



B. Audit Reporting Workflows

Where do people wait for data? Which recurring reports follow the data → analysis → deliverable pattern and could be automated?



C. Scope a 30-Day Sprint

Pick one team, one data source, 30 days. Build a prototype and test with real questions from real users.



D. Inventory Documents

Where is knowledge trapped? Policies, contracts, procedures — which collections would benefit from AI-powered Q&A?



E. Establish Governance

Who validates AI output? Who approves deployment? Define this before shadow IT does it for you.



You don’t need a 12-month roadmap. You need one pilot, one owner, one deadline.


Five things every participant can do regardless of role, starting tomorrow morning. You do not need a twelve-month roadmap. You need one pilot, one owner, one deadline.
A: Use AI for your personal workflow. The tools from Sessions 1 and 2 are available right now, no IT approval needed. Charts, documents, analyses, automated workflows. Start using them for your own work this week. This is the lowest-friction, highest-impact action on this list.
B: Audit your reporting workflows. Where do people wait for data? Which recurring reports follow the data to analysis to deliverable pattern and could be automated? Those are your automation candidates. We covered this in Session 4.
C: Scope a thirty-day discovery sprint. Pick one team, one data source, thirty days. Build a prototype and test it with real questions from real users. Not a proof of concept that sits on a shelf — a working prototype that real people use and give feedback on. Several of the strategies we heard today are structured exactly this way.
D: Inventory your document collections. Where is knowledge trapped? This was your breakout exercise in Session 7. Policies, contracts, procedures — which collections would benefit from AI-powered Q&A? You probably already have a list from last session.
E: Establish governance before shadow IT does it for you. Who validates AI output? Who approves deployment? If you do not define this, someone in your organization will deploy ChatGPT on sensitive company data without any guardrails, and you will be cleaning up the mess. Get ahead of it. This was the core message of Session 6.
Read the explainer. One pilot, one owner, one deadline. That is the formula. The companies that succeed with AI are not the ones with the biggest budgets or the most sophisticated technology. They are the ones that start small, learn fast, and scale what works.
[TIMING: About 2 minutes on this slide, from minute 86 to minute 88. Be crisp. These are action items, not a lecture. You are in the closing sequence now.]

title: Session 8: Your AI Strategy
text:
The 30-Day Sprint



Week 1


Pick & Prep


Choose one use case. Identify the data source. Get stakeholder buy-in.




Week 2


Build & Test


Connect the data. Build the prototype. Test with real questions from real users.




Week 3


Validate


Compare AI answers to known results. Document accuracy. Identify failure modes.




Week 4


Decide


Go/no-go for expanded pilot. Present findings. Define Phase 2 scope.




If you are going to run that thirty-day sprint, here is a week-by-week framework. Week 1: pick one use case, identify the data source, get stakeholder buy-in. The most important thing in Week 1 is the stakeholder conversation. If you do not have a business owner who cares about the outcome, the pilot will stall.
Week 2: connect the data, build the prototype, and start testing with real questions from real users. Not hypothetical questions — real questions that people actually need answered. If you are doing document AI, load the actual contracts or policies. If you are doing database AI, connect to the actual data source.
Week 3: validate. Compare AI answers to known results. Document accuracy and identify failure modes. This is the Session 6 maker/checker framework applied to your pilot. How often is the AI right? How often is it wrong? What kinds of questions does it handle well, and where does it break down?
Week 4: decide. Go or no-go for an expanded pilot. Present your findings to stakeholders. Define the Phase 2 scope based on what you learned. The decision is not “should we use AI” — the decision is “should we expand this specific use case to more users, more data, or more systems.”
This framework is deliberately simple. Complexity is the enemy of execution. Keep it tight, keep it moving, and learn as you go.
[TIMING: About 1 minute on this slide. It is a visual reference for the sprint framework. Do not belabor each week — the slide is self-explanatory. You should be at about minute 89.]

title: Session 8: Your AI Strategy
text:
Final Takeaway

One last thought. This is what I want you to remember when everything else fades.
[TIMING: Brief pause on the divider. Then advance to the closing statement. Let the moment breathe.]
