title: Session 2: Iterate, Critique, Trust
text:
Homework Debrief
What worked? What didn’t?

Welcome back to Session 2. Last session you used Claude for the first time — charts, documents, calculators. This week you used it on real work. Let us start by hearing what happened. I want to hear both the wins and the failures, because we learn more from the failures than from the successes. I will ask three or four of you to share.

title: Session 2: Iterate, Critique, Trust
text:
Share Your Best and Worst


Your Best Output

What task did you give Claude?
How good was the first draft?
How much editing did it need?
How much time did it save?



Your Worst Output

What went wrong?
Was it factually incorrect?
Was it generic or off-target?
What would have fixed it?




As people share, listen for patterns. Where does AI save the most time? Where does it need the most editing? And critically — when it fails, why does it fail? Is it getting facts wrong? Is it being too generic? Is it misunderstanding the audience? These patterns will tell us exactly where today’s techniques make the biggest difference.

title: Session 2: Iterate, Critique, Trust
text:
Patterns from Your Experience
Where did AI save time? Where did it need heavy editing?

Let me highlight a few patterns I am hearing. First, AI tends to be very good at structure and format — it produces well-organized documents quickly. Second, it tends to be generic when you give it generic instructions. Third, it sometimes gets facts wrong or invents plausible-sounding numbers. These are the exact problems that today’s techniques are designed to solve. Specificity beats generic prompting. Self-critique catches errors. And calibrated trust tells you how much checking to do.

title: Session 2: Iterate, Critique, Trust
text:
Today’s Agenda


🔄 Iterate Turn mediocre drafts into great ones


✅ Trust Calibrate verification to stakes


🛠️ Skills Build reusable prompts and workflows


📋 Build Create your own recurring workflow



Here is what we are covering today. First, the art of iteration — how to take a mediocre first draft and make it genuinely useful through specific, directed feedback. Second, the maker/checker framework formalized — calibrating your level of trust to the stakes of the task. Third, skills and structured prompts — how to build reusable instructions that produce consistent output every time. And fourth, hands-on work where you build a reusable workflow for a recurring task in your own job.

title: Session 2: Iterate, Critique, Trust
text:
Demo: The Art of Iteration

Let me start with a demo. I am going to take a mediocre first draft — either from the homework debrief or a prepared example — and show you the iteration cycle that turns it into something genuinely good. Pay attention to the specificity of my directions. That is the skill.

title: Session 2: Iterate, Critique, Trust
text:
The Iteration Cycle


📄 Draft Get the first version — fast but mediocre


🎯 Direct Give specific feedback, not “make it better”


🔍 Self-Critique Ask Claude to find its own weaknesses


✨ Fix Apply the critique — dramatically better output



Here is the cycle. Step one: get a first draft. It will be fast but usually mediocre — too generic, too long, missing nuance. Step two: give specific direction. Not “make it better” but “make it specific to our Q4 results” or “the chart is misleading — use a zero baseline” or “rewrite the executive summary in half the length.” Step three — and this is the power move — ask Claude to critique its own work. “What is weak about this analysis? What would a skeptical CFO push back on?” Step four: tell it to fix those issues. Two rounds of self-critique produce dramatically better output than any amount of vague “improve this” prompting.

title: Session 2: Iterate, Critique, Trust
text:
LIVE DEMO
From mediocre draft to polished output

Let me show this live. I am going to take a mediocre executive summary — either from someone’s homework or my prepared example — and run through the full iteration cycle. Watch how each round of specific feedback transforms the output. And pay special attention to the self-critique step — when Claude identifies its own weaknesses, it often catches things we would not have thought to ask about.

title: Session 2: Iterate, Critique, Trust
text:
What Just Happened

The iteration sequence
Round 1: “This is too generic — make it specific to our Q4 results.” → Output improves from template to tailored.
Round 2: “The chart is misleading — use a zero baseline.” → Visual accuracy corrected.
Round 3: “Rewrite the executive summary in half the length.” → Concise, focused, executive-ready.
Round 4: “What’s weak about this analysis? What would a skeptical CFO push back on?” → Claude identifies 3-4 real gaps.
Round 5: “Fix those issues.” → Final version is dramatically stronger.


Let me recap what we just did. Round one: we replaced generic language with specific details. Round two: we fixed a misleading visualization. Round three: we cut the length in half, which forced the AI to prioritize. Round four: we asked Claude to critique itself — and it found real problems. Round five: we fixed those problems. Each round took maybe 30 seconds. The total investment was about three minutes, and the difference between the first draft and the final version is night and day. This is the skill. Specific direction, self-critique, and iteration.

title: Session 2: Iterate, Critique, Trust
text:
The Secret: Specificity


Vague (Weak)

“Make it better”
“Improve the analysis”
“Make it more professional”
“Fix the chart”
“Add more detail”



Specific (Strong)

“Lead with the biggest risk to Q4 revenue”
“Add year-over-year comparisons for each metric”
“Cut the background section and start with the recommendation”
“Use a zero baseline and add data labels”
“Include the three customers that drove the variance”




Here is the core principle. Vague feedback produces vague improvement. Specific feedback produces specific improvement. When you say “make it better,” Claude has to guess what you mean. When you say “lead with the biggest risk to Q4 revenue,” it knows exactly what to do. This is true of managing people too, by the way — but it is even more true of managing AI, because AI has no context about your preferences, your audience, or your organizational culture unless you tell it explicitly.

title: Session 2: Iterate, Critique, Trust
text:
The Maker/Checker Framework

Now let me formalize something we previewed at the end of Session 1. The maker/checker framework. This is how you think about every AI output for the rest of this course — and for the rest of your career using these tools.

title: Session 2: Iterate, Critique, Trust
text:
The Core Shift

The professional skill is shifting:
From producing analysis → To directing and verifying it
AI is the maker. You are the checker.


The fundamental shift is this: the professional skill used to be producing analysis. Building the spreadsheet, writing the report, creating the presentation. Now the skill is directing and verifying. You tell the AI what to make, and then you check whether it made it correctly. This is not a lesser skill — it requires deep domain expertise, judgment, and an understanding of what “correct” means in context. But it is a different skill, and developing it is what this course is about.

title: Session 2: Iterate, Critique, Trust
text:
Calibrated Trust


🟢 Low Stakes

Internal notes
Brainstorming sessions
Personal exploration
First-draft ideas

Verification: Quick sanity check. Glance at axes, skim for obvious errors.


🟡 Medium Stakes

Team presentations
Internal reports
Meeting summaries
Analysis for your manager

Verification: Verify every number. Check that framing matches reality. Test key claims.


🔴 High Stakes

Board presentations
Regulatory filings
Public-facing documents
Financial disclosures

Verification: Independent verification of every claim. Cross-check sources. Second reviewer.



Calibrated trust means matching your verification effort to the consequences of being wrong. Low stakes: internal notes, brainstorming, personal explorations. If the chart has a minor error, nobody is hurt. A quick sanity check is sufficient. Medium stakes: team presentations, internal reports, analysis for your manager. If the numbers are wrong, your credibility takes a hit. Verify every number. Check that the framing matches what you know to be true. High stakes: board presentations, regulatory filings, anything public-facing. If these are wrong, the consequences are severe. Every claim needs independent verification. You should cross-check sources and have a second reviewer. The right answer is not “always check everything” — that defeats the purpose of using AI. The right answer is “check proportionally to what happens if it is wrong.”

title: Session 2: Iterate, Critique, Trust
text:
The Critical Question
What happens if this output is wrong?
If the answer is “nothing much” → quick check.
If the answer is “serious consequences” → full verification.

This is the question to ask yourself every single time you use AI output in your work. What happens if this is wrong? If the answer is “nothing much” — it is a personal brainstorm, a draft for your own thinking — then a quick check is fine. If the answer is “serious consequences” — reputational damage, financial loss, regulatory exposure — then you need full verification. This is not about being paranoid. It is about being professional. You would not let a junior analyst present unchecked numbers to the board. Do not let an AI do it either.

title: Session 2: Iterate, Critique, Trust
text:
Demo: Skills and Structured Prompts

Now let me show you something that takes your productivity to the next level. So far, every time you use Claude, you start from scratch. You describe what you want, you iterate, you get a good result. But what if you do the same type of task every week? A status report, a data analysis, a document review? You should not have to re-explain what you want every time. That is where skills and structured prompts come in.

title: Session 2: Iterate, Critique, Trust
text:
What Is a Skill?

A skill is a reusable set of instructions that produces consistent output every time.
Write it once. Reuse it every week. Get the same quality without re-explaining.


Think of it as a template for your AI — not a document template with blanks to fill in, but an instruction set that tells the AI exactly what to produce, in what format, with what tone and structure.


A skill is a reusable set of instructions. Instead of telling Claude what you want from scratch every time, you write a detailed prompt once — specifying the sections, the format, the tone, the level of detail — and then you reuse it. Every time you run the skill, you get consistent, high-quality output without having to re-explain your preferences. Think of it as the difference between giving a new employee instructions every day versus writing them a standard operating procedure.

title: Session 2: Iterate, Critique, Trust
text:
Example: Weekly Status Report Skill

Reusable prompt
You are a business analyst preparing a weekly status report.
Format: Start with a 2-sentence executive summary. Then three sections: 1. Key Accomplishments (bullet points, max 5) 2. Risks and Blockers (bullet points with severity: high/medium/low) 3. Next Week Priorities (numbered list, max 4)
Tone: Direct, factual, no filler. Write for a VP who reads 20 of these. Length: One page maximum. Always end with: “Decision needed:” followed by any open items requiring management input, or “None this week.”


Here is a concrete example. This is a weekly status report skill. It specifies exactly what Claude should produce: a two-sentence executive summary, three sections with specific formats, a direct tone written for a VP who reads twenty of these, a one-page maximum, and a closing section for decisions needed. You write this once. Then every week, you paste in your notes and run the skill. Claude produces a consistent, well-formatted status report in seconds. The quality is repeatable because the instructions are explicit.

title: Session 2: Iterate, Critique, Trust
section: The Power Move: Sub-Agents and Critique
text:
The Power Move: Sub-Agents and Critique


Drafter

Produces the initial output
Follows the skill instructions
Optimizes for completeness
Works fast



Critic

Reviews the draft independently
Checks for accuracy and gaps
Identifies weak arguments
Flags unsupported claims



Fact-Checker

Verifies specific numbers
Cross-references data
Catches inconsistencies
Confirms calculations




Here is where it gets really powerful. Instead of one AI doing everything, you can use sub-agents — different AI roles working in parallel. One drafts. One critiques. One fact-checks. This is the slash-critique pattern: you ask Claude to generate three independent reviews of its own output, each from a different perspective. A drafter focuses on completeness. A critic looks for weak arguments and gaps. A fact-checker verifies numbers and catches inconsistencies. The result is dramatically more reliable than a single-pass output.

title: Session 2: Iterate, Critique, Trust
text:
LIVE DEMO
Skills, structured prompts, and the /critique pattern

Let me show you this live. I am going to create a skill for a weekly status report, run it with sample data, and then show the critique pattern. Watch how the structured prompt produces consistent output, and how the self-critique step catches issues that the initial draft missed. This is the workflow you will build for yourselves in the hands-on session.

title: Session 2: Iterate, Critique, Trust
text:
What You Just Saw

The workflow

Skill created: Reusable instructions for a recurring task
Consistent output: Same format, tone, and structure every time
Sub-agent critique: Three independent reviewers catch different issues
Final version: Incorporates all critique — stronger than any single pass



Let me recap. We created a reusable skill — a set of instructions that produces consistent output every time. We ran it and got a well-formatted status report in seconds. Then we used the critique pattern — three independent reviewers examining the output from different angles. The final version incorporated all the feedback and was significantly stronger than the first draft. This entire workflow took maybe two minutes and produced something that would normally take thirty to forty-five minutes of writing and editing.

title: Session 2: Iterate, Critique, Trust
text:
Hands-On: Build Your Own Workflow

Now it is your turn. I want each of you to pick a recurring task from your work and build a reusable workflow for it. This is not an exercise — this is something you will actually use starting this week. Think about what you do repeatedly: weekly reports, data analysis, document reviews, meeting summaries. Pick one and build a skill for it.

title: Session 2: Iterate, Critique, Trust
text:
Choose Your Workflow


Option 1: Report Template

Pick a recurring report you write
Define the sections, format, and tone
Specify what data goes in each section
Build a reusable prompt
Test it with real or sample data



Option 2: Data Analysis

Define a standard analysis pattern
“Given a CSV, always produce these 5 charts and a summary”
Specify chart types and formatting
Include interpretation guidance
Test with the sample dataset



Option 3: Document Review

Build a review checklist
“Read this document and flag issues in these categories”
Specify severity levels
Include tone and audience checks
Test with a real document




Here are three options, but you are not limited to these. Option one: build a template for a recurring report — a weekly status, a monthly review, a quarterly summary. Define the sections, the format, the tone. Option two: build a data analysis workflow — “given a CSV, always produce these five charts and a summary paragraph.” Option three: build a document review checklist — “read this document and flag issues in these categories with severity levels.” Pick whichever fits your work best, and use the critique technique to refine it. You have about fifteen minutes.

title: Session 2: Iterate, Critique, Trust
text:
Tips for Building Your Workflow

What makes a good skill

Be explicit about format: sections, length, bullet vs. paragraph, headers
Specify tone: formal vs. conversational, direct vs. diplomatic
Define the audience: who reads this? What do they care about?
Include examples: “A good bullet point looks like this: …”
Add constraints: “Never exceed one page” or “Always include a recommendation”



Test it 2-3 times with different inputs. A good skill works consistently, not just once.


A few tips as you build. Be explicit about format — specify sections, length limits, whether you want bullets or paragraphs, what the headers should be. Specify the tone — formal or conversational, direct or diplomatic. Define the audience — who reads this and what do they care about? Include examples of what good output looks like. And add constraints — “never exceed one page” or “always include a recommendation.” Then test it two or three times with different inputs. A skill that only works once is not a skill — it should produce consistent quality across different inputs.

title: Session 2: Iterate, Critique, Trust
text:
Breakout: Your Productivity Portfolio
Groups of 3-4

Let me move you into breakout rooms. Each person will share the workflow they built. I want you to compare: what types of tasks came up? What patterns are common across your work? And critically — what level of trust does each task require? Use the low, medium, high framework we discussed earlier.

title: Session 2: Iterate, Critique, Trust
text:
Breakout Discussion Guide


Share and Compare

What workflow did you build?
What recurring task does it address?
How much time does it save?
What trust level does it require?



Identify Top Use Cases

Which workflows save the most time?
Which have acceptable risk levels?
What patterns are common?
Each group: pick your top 3 highest-value use cases




Here is your discussion guide. First, each person shares what they built. Then compare: what types of tasks came up? Are there common patterns? And here is the key question: for each workflow, what level of trust does it require? A brainstorming skill is low stakes — you barely need to check. A board report skill is high stakes — you check everything. Your group should identify the top three use cases where AI saves the most time with acceptable risk. That is your productivity portfolio — the set of tasks where AI gives you the biggest return on your checking investment.

title: Session 2: Iterate, Critique, Trust
text:
Group Reports
Each group: your top 3 highest-value AI use cases.

Welcome back. Let me hear from each group. What were your top three highest-value use cases — the tasks where AI saves the most time with acceptable risk? I am looking for patterns across groups.

title: Session 2: Iterate, Critique, Trust
text:
Bridge to Enterprise Data

Everything you have done so far in this course uses data you paste in or documents you upload. You are bringing the data to the AI. But most of the data that drives business decisions does not live in your clipboard — it lives in enterprise systems. CRMs, ERPs, HR platforms, financial systems. Starting next session, we flip the model. Instead of you bringing data to the AI, the AI goes to the data.

title: Session 2: Iterate, Critique, Trust
text:
The Shift Ahead


Sessions 1-2: You bring the data

Copy-paste into Claude
Upload documents
Manual data entry
Limited to what you can carry
Great for personal productivity



Sessions 3+: AI goes to the data

AI queries enterprise databases
CRM, ERP, HR, finance systems
Cross-system analysis in seconds
The same skills — iteration, critique, verification
Organizational-scale impact




Here is the shift. In Sessions 1 and 2, you brought the data to the AI — you pasted tables, uploaded documents, typed in numbers. That is great for personal productivity, and you should keep doing it. But starting in Session 3, the AI goes to the data. It queries Salesforce for your pipeline, NetSuite for your financials, Workday for your headcount — and it does it from a single chat interface. The skills you have developed — iteration, critique, calibrated trust — remain exactly the same. But the scope expands from your personal work to your organization’s data.

title: Session 2: Iterate, Critique, Trust

Preview: Meridian Corp

A simulated enterprise environment

$500M B2B industrial supplies distributor with three operating divisions
10 enterprise systems: Salesforce, NetSuite, SAP, Workday, HubSpot, Oracle, and more
41 tables, 26,000+ rows, 3 years of data
Ask questions in plain English — the AI queries the databases for you

Next session you will meet Meridian Corp — a simulated $500 million B2B industrial supplies distributor. It has three operating divisions, ten enterprise systems, 41 tables, and over 26,000 rows of realistic data spanning three years. You will log into a web application, ask questions in plain English, and watch the AI query multiple databases, merge the results, and produce charts and narratives. It is exactly like what you have been doing in Sessions 1 and 2 — but now the data comes from enterprise systems instead of your clipboard. And here is the key: the same iteration, critique, and verification skills you practiced today are exactly what you will use when the AI is querying databases. The stakes just get higher.

title: Session 1: What AI Can Do for You
text:
Welcome

Welcome everyone to Session 1 of Business Intelligence and AI. I am excited to have you here. Over the next four weeks, we are going to transform the way you think about using AI in your daily work — and then extend that to enterprise data and organizational strategy. Today, we start with you. Personal productivity. Immediate value. By the time you leave this session, you will have used AI to produce real deliverables.

title: Session 1: What AI Can Do for You
section: What We Covered in the Pre-Program Video
text:
What We Covered in the Pre-Program Video

Quick recap

Course tools: Claude.ai (browser), Claude Desktop (local files), Claude Code (building tools)
The arc: personal productivity → enterprise data → deployment → strategy
Meridian Corp: a $500M distributor simulation — starting in Week 2
By Session 8: a concrete AI strategy for your organization



Before we dive in, let me quickly recap what you saw in the pre-program video. You learned about the three tools we will use — Claude in the browser, Claude Desktop for working with your own files, and Claude Code which I will use to build things in later sessions. You saw the overall arc of the course: we start with personal productivity, move to enterprise data in Week 2, then cover deployment and governance, and finish with strategy. And you got a preview of Meridian Corp, the simulated company we will use starting in Session 3. Today, though, it is all about you and your immediate productivity.

title: Session 1: What AI Can Do for You
text:
The “Why Now”
In February 2026, AI agents wiped $2 trillion from software company valuations.

So why does this matter right now? Let me set the context. In February 2026, something remarkable happened. AI agents — software that can understand plain English and take action — triggered the largest repricing in software history. Two trillion dollars in market value disappeared in a matter of weeks. The market sent a very clear signal: AI can replace large categories of enterprise software. Whether or not you agree with the market’s verdict, the technology behind it is real, and it is moving fast.

title: Session 1: What AI Can Do for You
text:
The SAASpocalypse

“The AI agent revolution is wreaking havoc on traditional SaaS valuations. Investors are betting that intelligent agents will replace the click-heavy workflows that enterprise software companies have spent decades building.”


— CNBC, “Software stocks face worst rout in a decade as AI agents threaten business models,” February 2026


The press called it the SAASpocalypse. CNBC, TechCrunch, and every major financial outlet covered it. The thesis was simple: if an AI agent can do what a human does inside Salesforce, or SAP, or ServiceNow — querying data, generating reports, automating workflows — then why pay per-seat license fees for the software itself? The market decided that the answer, for many categories, is: you will not need to.

title: Session 1: What AI Can Do for You
text:
The Damage


−31% Salesforce


−32% Adobe


−35% Atlassian



Here are some of the numbers. Salesforce, the world’s largest CRM company, lost 31 percent of its value. Adobe, which dominates creative software, dropped 32 percent. Atlassian, the maker of Jira and Confluence — tools used by virtually every software team — fell 35 percent. These are not small companies. These are pillars of the enterprise software industry, and the market is telling them their moats may be thinner than they thought.

title: Session 1: What AI Can Do for You
text:
What AI Is Replacing


The Interface Layer

Click-heavy workflows
Form-filling and navigation
Manual data entry across screens
“Training” users on software



Workflow Automation

Rule-based routing
Report scheduling
Template-driven outputs
Repetitive analysis tasks



Per-Seat Economics

$150-300/user/month SaaS fees
Shelfware (paid but unused)
Feature bloat nobody asked for
Integration tax between tools




So what exactly is AI replacing? Three things. First, the interface layer — all those clicks, forms, and navigation that enterprise software forces you through. If you can just say what you want in English, the UI becomes unnecessary. Second, workflow automation — rules-based routing, report scheduling, template-driven outputs. AI handles these natively. Third, the per-seat economics model itself. Companies pay 150 to 300 dollars per user per month for SaaS tools, and much of that capacity goes unused. AI collapses the cost structure.

title: Session 1: What AI Can Do for You
text:
What Survives
Data
The systems of record. The databases. The institutional knowledge embedded in your CRM, ERP, and HR systems.
AI does not replace your data — it makes it accessible.

But here is what does not get replaced: data. Your CRM still holds your customer relationships. Your ERP still tracks your supply chain. Your HR system still manages your people. AI does not replace those systems of record. What it does is make the data inside them dramatically more accessible. Instead of needing a BI team to build a dashboard, you ask a question in English. That is the shift — and it is why we spend the second half of this course on enterprise data.

title: Session 1: What AI Can Do for You
section: But This Course Starts With You
text:
But This Course Starts With You
Before AI transforms your organization, it can transform your Tuesday afternoon.

But we are not starting with enterprise systems. We are starting with something much more immediate. Before AI transforms your organization, it can transform your Tuesday afternoon. The personal productivity gains are available to you right now, today, with no IT department involvement, no deployment project, no budget approval. That is where we begin.

title: Session 1: What AI Can Do for You
text:
Demo: AI as Your Analyst

Let me show you what I mean. I am going to open Claude.ai right now and produce three deliverables in about three minutes. Watch the speed, watch the format flexibility, and pay attention to the quality. Then ask yourself: how long would this have taken you?

text:
What You Are About to See


📊 Chart Paste numbers, get an interactive visualization


📝 Memo Turn the data into an executive summary


📑 Slide Reformat as a PowerPoint-ready slide


⚡ 3 Minutes Three deliverables, one conversation



Here is the sequence. First, I will paste a table of quarterly sales numbers and ask Claude to generate a chart. You will see an interactive artifact — you can hover over data points, filter, and export. Second, I will ask it to turn the same data into an executive summary memo. Third, I will ask for a PowerPoint-ready slide with bullet points. Three deliverables, three minutes, one conversation. This is what I mean by AI as your analyst — it drafts, you direct and verify.

title: Session 1: What AI Can Do for You
text:
The Key Framing

AI is a fast junior analyst.
It drafts. You direct and verify.
It is not replacing your judgment — it is accelerating your output.


I want you to hold onto this framing throughout the entire course. AI is a fast junior analyst. It can produce a first draft of almost anything in seconds. But like any junior analyst, it needs direction, it sometimes makes mistakes, and you are the one who decides whether the output is good enough to use. Your judgment, your domain expertise, your understanding of the audience — that is what turns an AI draft into a real deliverable.

title: Session 1: What AI Can Do for You
text:
And It Produces Real Files


Excel Workbooks
Formatted .xlsx files with formulas, multiple sheets, charts, and conditional formatting


Word Documents
Professional .docx files with headings, tables, page breaks, and styled formatting


PowerPoint Decks
.pptx slide decks with layouts, bullet points, charts, and speaker notes


Charts & Dashboards
Interactive visualizations, PDF reports, CSV exports — whatever format the task requires



These are not screenshots or mockups. AI generates actual files you can open, edit, and send. An executive summary as a Word doc. A financial model as an Excel workbook. A board deck as PowerPoint. We will produce all of these in this course.


I want to emphasize something important. What Claude produces are not just text responses in a chat window. It generates actual files. Real Excel workbooks with formulas and multiple sheets. Real Word documents with proper formatting. Real PowerPoint decks with layouts and charts. You can download them, open them, edit them, and send them to your colleagues. In this course, we will produce all of these formats. The Meridian Corp app you will use starting in Session 3 generates these same file types from enterprise data.

title: Session 1: What AI Can Do for You
section: How AI + Code + Data Work Together
text:
How AI + Code + Data Work Together

Now let me explain something important about how these systems actually work. Understanding this architecture will help you make sense of everything we cover in this course — from personal productivity today to enterprise deployment in later sessions.

title: Session 1: What AI Can Do for You
text:
Three Moving Pieces


The AI Brain

The large language model (LLM)
Always in the cloud — hosted by Anthropic, OpenAI, Google, etc.
Understands your question
Generates text, code, and plans
Has no permanent memory



The Data

Could be on your computer (spreadsheets, local files)
Could be in SaaS provider storage (Salesforce, Workday, NetSuite)
Could be on company-managed servers (on-prem or cloud)
Where data lives determines security requirements



Code Execution

Something has to run the Python, SQL, or scripts the AI writes
Could run on the LLM provider’s hardware (Claude.ai sandbox)
Could run on company servers (Docker, on-prem)
Could run on your computer (Claude Desktop, terminal)




There are three moving pieces in every AI system, and understanding where each one lives is the key to understanding the whole course. First, the AI brain — the large language model. This is always in the cloud, hosted by the provider. It understands your question, generates text and code, but has no permanent memory. Second, the data. This could be on your own computer — a spreadsheet, a PDF. It could be in SaaS provider storage — your Salesforce CRM, your Workday HR system. Or it could be on company-managed servers. Where your data lives determines the security requirements. Third, code execution. When the AI writes Python or SQL to analyze data, something has to actually run that code. It could run on the LLM provider’s hardware — that is what happens when you use Claude.ai’s sandbox. It could run on your company’s servers. Or it could run on your own computer. Each combination has different implications for security, cost, and capability.

title: Session 1: What AI Can Do for You
section: Today vs. Later in This Course
text:
Today vs. Later in This Course










Sessions 1-2
Sessions 3-7




AI Brain
Claude (Anthropic cloud)
Claude (Anthropic cloud)


Data
You paste it in or upload it
Meridian Corp databases (10 systems)


Code Execution
Claude.ai sandbox (Anthropic’s servers)
Meridian app server / Linux VM


Security concern
Low — your own data, your choice
High — enterprise data, access control needed


Your role
Direct and verify output
Direct, verify, AND govern access





This week, all three pieces are simple: Claude’s cloud brain, your data pasted in, code running in Claude’s sandbox. Starting Session 3, the data lives in enterprise systems and the security questions get real. That progression is the arc of this course.


Here is how this maps to our course. This week — Sessions 1 and 2 — it is simple. The AI is Claude in the cloud. The data is whatever you paste in or upload. The code runs in Claude’s sandbox on Anthropic’s servers. The security concern is low because it is your own data and you are choosing to share it. Starting in Session 3, things get more interesting. The data lives in enterprise databases — CRM, ERP, HR systems. The code execution moves to dedicated servers. And now the security questions get real: who can access what data? Where does it travel? Who is liable if something goes wrong? That progression — from personal productivity with simple architecture to enterprise deployment with complex architecture — is the arc of the entire course. Sessions 5 and 6 are entirely about those deployment and governance decisions.

title: Session 1: What AI Can Do for You
text:
Hands-On: Your First Artifacts

Time for you to try it yourselves. We have three guided exercises, each demonstrating a different type of artifact Claude can create. I will walk you through the first one, and then you will have time to work through all three at your own pace. Open claude.ai in your browser now if you have not already.

title: Session 1: What AI Can Do for You
text:
Exercise 1: Build a Chart

Instructions

Paste the sample dataset (provided in chat) into Claude.ai
Ask: “Create a bar chart comparing Q1 through Q4 revenue by product line”
Iterate: “Change the colors to blue and gray” → “Add data labels” → “Switch to a line chart” → “Make it a stacked bar chart”
Notice the interactive artifact — hover, filter, explore



The point: You are not learning to code. You are learning to direct. Tell it what you want, see the result, refine with plain English.


Exercise one is about visualization. I have pasted a sample dataset into the chat — quarterly revenue for three product lines. Copy it into Claude.ai and ask for a bar chart. But do not stop at the first version. This is where the real skill is. Ask it to change colors, add labels, switch chart types. Try “make it a stacked bar chart” or “add a trend line.” Each iteration takes seconds, and you are learning the most important skill in working with AI: directing and refining. You are not coding. You are describing what you want and adjusting until it is right.

title: Session 1: What AI Can Do for You
text:
Exercise 2: Build an Interactive Calculator

Instructions

Ask Claude: “Build me an interactive break-even analysis calculator”
It should have inputs for: fixed costs, variable cost per unit, selling price per unit
Iterate: “Add a chart showing profit/loss across different volumes” → “Add a sensitivity table” → “Make the inputs sliders instead of text boxes”
Try your own: ROI calculator, loan amortization, pricing model



Exercise two is about interactive tools. Ask Claude to build a break-even calculator. It will create a working application right in the chat — with input fields, calculations, and output. Then iterate: ask for a chart, a sensitivity table, sliders instead of text boxes. The artifact is fully interactive, and you can keep refining it. If break-even does not fit your work, try an ROI calculator, a loan amortization schedule, or a pricing model. The point is that Claude can build functional tools, not just static documents.

title: Session 1: What AI Can Do for You
text:
Exercise 3: Draft an Executive Summary

Instructions

Open the briefing document (provided) — a two-page quarterly performance update
Paste it into Claude and ask: “Write a one-page executive summary for the CEO”
Iterate: “Make it half the length” → “Lead with the biggest risk” → “Add a recommendation section” → “Change the tone to be more direct”
Compare your iterations — notice how specific direction produces specific improvement



Exercise three is about written documents. I have provided a two-page quarterly briefing document. Paste it into Claude and ask for a one-page executive summary for the CEO. Then iterate — and pay close attention to how the specificity of your direction determines the quality of the output. Saying “make it better” gets you almost nothing. Saying “lead with the biggest risk and cut the background section” gets you a dramatically different and usually better document. This is the core skill: specific direction produces specific improvement.

title: Session 1: What AI Can Do for You
text:
Open Exploration
Bring something from your actual work.

Now I want you to go beyond the exercises. For the next ten minutes, try Claude with something from your actual work. A dataset you have been meaning to analyze. A document you need to draft. A summary you have been putting off. A comparison you need to make. The exercises showed you what is possible — now apply it to something real. If you produce something interesting, I will ask you to share it with the group. And if you get stuck, raise your hand or drop a message in the chat.

Here are some ideas if you need a starting point. You can analyze — paste a dataset and ask for trends, comparisons, or charts. You can draft — an email, a meeting summary, a project proposal. You can summarize — take a long document and ask for key points or an executive brief. Or you can build — a calculator, a comparison matrix, a decision framework. All of these are things Claude handles well, and all of them are things that typically take you 30 minutes to an hour but take Claude about 30 seconds for a first draft.

Here are your three discussion questions. First: what surprised you? Both on the upside and the downside. Second: what would you actually use this for tomorrow? Not in theory — in practice, this week, at your desk. And third: what patterns do you see? What tasks in your work follow a repeatable pattern where you spend more time on structure and formatting than on actual thinking? Those are the tasks where AI delivers the most value. Welcome back. Let me hear from each group — one insight, one thing that came up in your discussion that you think the rest of the class should hear. We will go quickly around the room.

title: Session 1: What AI Can Do for You
text:
The Maker/Checker Preview


The Maker

AI generates the first draft
Fast, tireless, format-flexible
No domain expertise
No sense of stakes or audience
Will confidently produce wrong answers



The Checker

You verify, refine, and approve
Domain knowledge and judgment
Understands the audience
Knows what “wrong” costs
The irreplaceable human role




Before we wrap up, I want to plant a seed that will grow throughout this entire course. Everything AI produces needs verification. Everything. The question is not whether to check — it is how much to check. And the answer depends on the stakes. This is the maker/checker framework. AI is the maker — fast, tireless, format-flexible, but with no domain expertise and no sense of what getting it wrong would cost. You are the checker — you bring judgment, domain knowledge, and an understanding of your audience. We will formalize this framework next session.

title: Session 1: What AI Can Do for You
text:
The Spectrum of Trust


🟢 Low Stakes Personal notes, brainstorming, exploration


🟡 Medium Stakes Team decks, internal reports, summaries


🔴 High Stakes Board decks, regulatory filings, public docs


🎯 Your Judgment Match verification effort to consequences



Think of it as a spectrum. Green: low stakes — personal notes, brainstorming, quick explorations. A fast sanity check is enough. Yellow: medium stakes — team presentations, internal reports, meeting summaries. You need to verify numbers and check that the framing is accurate. Red: high stakes — board decks, regulatory filings, anything public-facing. Every claim, every number, every source needs independent verification. And the skill you are developing is judgment — knowing where each output falls on this spectrum and calibrating your verification effort accordingly. Next session, we formalize this into a framework you can apply to every AI interaction.

A chart you actually need, a document you actually have to draft, an analysis you actually have to do. Save or screenshot the outputs. Second, for each task, write down what was good, what needed editing, and how long it would have taken you without AI. Next session we go deeper. You will learn the art of iteration — how to take a mediocre first draft and turn it into something genuinely useful through specific, directed feedback. After that, in Session 3, we connect AI to enterprise data for the first time, and the rest of the course builds from there. But it all starts with the personal productivity skills you practiced today. See you next session.

title: Session 3: AI Meets Your Data
section: From Personal Productivity to Enterprise Data
text:
From Personal Productivity to Enterprise Data

This section divider opens the bridge segment (minutes 0-8). You are transitioning the class from “personal AI” mode — where they spent a week generating charts, documents, and workflows with Claude — into “enterprise AI” mode, where the same technology connects to corporate databases. Let the title land, then advance.

title: Session 3: AI Meets Your Data
text:
The Shift

You have spent a week using AI for personal productivity — charts, documents, workflows. Now we scale up. The same AI that drafts your memos can also query your company’s databases.


But enterprise data is scattered across 5, 10, sometimes 20 systems. Today we see what that looks like — and what AI can do about it.


Say: “You have spent a week using AI for personal productivity — charts, documents, workflows. You have iterated, critiqued, and built reusable prompts. Now we scale up. The same AI that drafts your memos can also query your company’s databases. But enterprise data is scattered across many systems. Today we see what that looks like and what AI can do about it.”
“By the end of this session, you will have queried enterprise databases in plain English, watched AI merge data across systems that don’t talk to each other, and started mapping your own company’s data landscape. Let’s jump right in.”
Transition: advance to the cold open.

title: Session 3: AI Meets Your Data
text:
The 3-Week Email Chain

This is the cold open provocation (minutes 8-13). The goal is to create an emotional anchor: the frustration of waiting weeks for a simple cross-system question. You will present the scenario and then immediately contrast it with the Meridian app demo.

title: Session 3: AI Meets Your Data
section: “Which of Our Customers Buy from Multiple Divisions?”
text:
“Which of Our Customers Buy from Multiple Divisions?”



The CEO’s Question


Simple enough to ask in one sentence
Requires data from 3 separate CRMs
Nobody owns the cross-system view
The answer should drive pricing strategy




The 3-Week Reality


Week 1: BI team extracts from Salesforce
Week 2: Energy team pulls from legacy CRM
Week 3: Manual matching in Excel, finally delivered
CEO has moved on to a different priority




Say: “Here is a scenario that might feel familiar. A CEO asks a simple question: which of our customers buy from multiple divisions? It is one sentence. It should be answerable. But here is what actually happens.”
Walk through the right card: “Week one, the BI team extracts data from Salesforce. Week two, someone from the Energy division pulls from their legacy CRM. Week three, an analyst is manually matching customer names in Excel. By the time the answer arrives, the CEO has moved on to a different priority.”
Ask the room: “Has this happened to you? A simple business question that took weeks because the data lived in different systems? Raise your hand if the answer is yes.”
Pause for responses. Then: “What I am about to show you takes about 30 seconds.”

text:

Introducing Meridian Corp


The Simulation Environment


$500M B2B industrial distributor with 3 operating divisions
10 enterprise systems: Salesforce, Legacy CRM, HubSpot, NetSuite, SAP, Oracle SCM, Workday, Zendesk, QuickBooks
41 tables, 26,000+ rows, 3 years of data
Realistic cross-system friction: different naming conventions, date formats, column styles



You will log in and query this environment yourself in 15 minutes. First, watch what happens when we ask it the CEO’s question.


Briefly introduce the Meridian Corp simulation. Say: “This is the environment you will be working with for the rest of the course. Meridian Corp is a fictional $500M B2B industrial supplies distributor. It has three divisions — Industrial, Energy, and Safety — each with their own CRM, plus shared finance, HR, and support systems. Ten systems total. Forty-one database tables. Three years of realistic data.”
“The key word is realistic. The systems have different naming conventions. The Legacy CRM stores dates as text strings. Customers appear under different names across divisions — General Electric in one system, GE Industrial Solutions in another. This is what real enterprise data looks like.”
“You will log in and query this yourself in a few minutes. But first, let me show you what happens when we ask it the CEO’s question.”
Switch to the Meridian app and prepare for the live demo.

title: Session 3: AI Meets Your Data
text:
Behind the Scenes



1


Query SF


Pull customers + revenue from Salesforce CRM




2


Query Legacy


Pull from Energy division’s custom CRM




3


Query HubSpot


Pull from Safety division’s CRM




4


Fuzzy Merge


Python matches names across systems (GE = General Electric)




The agent cannot join across systems in a single query. It pulls from each, then uses Python to reconcile. This mirrors how a real enterprise AI tool works.


Walk through each step card. Say: “What you just saw was not a single database query. The agent queried three separate systems, one at a time. It pulled customer data and revenue from Salesforce, then the Legacy CRM, then HubSpot. Then it used Python to merge the results.”
“The merge step is where the magic happens. The agent used fuzzy name matching to link ‘General Electric’ in Salesforce to ‘GE Industrial Solutions’ in the Legacy CRM to ‘GE Safety Division’ in HubSpot. An exact-match join would have missed 7 of the 10 multi-division customers.”
“This is important to understand: the agent cannot join across systems in a single SQL query. It queries each system where the data lives, then reconciles in code. This mirrors how a real enterprise AI tool would work — and it is different from a data warehouse, where you would move everything into one place first.”

text:
The Business Lens

This transitions to the business implications segment (minutes 23-33). You are connecting what they just saw to the SaaSpocalypse from Session 1 and to the broader strategic implications.

title: Session 3: AI Meets Your Data
text:
Remember the SaaSpocalypse?



What the Market Saw


AI agents querying enterprise data in plain English
Instant charts and narratives from a chat interface
Cross-system answers that used to require BI teams
$2 trillion in software market cap erased




What You Just Saw


The Meridian app answered in 30 seconds what took 3 weeks
Fuzzy matching across 3 CRMs with different naming conventions
Combined revenue view that no single dashboard could produce
This is the technology behind the valuation collapse




Say: “In Session 1, I told you about the SaaSpocalypse — AI agents wiped $2 trillion from software company valuations because the market believes this technology will displace dashboards, reporting teams, and data integration tools.”
“What you just saw is the technology behind that belief. An AI agent querying enterprise data in plain English, merging results across systems that do not talk to each other, and delivering an instant answer with charts and narrative.”
“Now, whether the market is right about the magnitude — that is debatable. But the capability is real. You just used it.”
Ask the room: “If anyone in your company could do what you just saw — ask a question in English and get an instant answer from your enterprise systems — what changes? What roles are affected? What processes become unnecessary?”

title: Session 3: AI Meets Your Data
text:
The Implication



Dashboards


Pre-built views of pre-selected metrics
Cannot answer questions they were not designed for
Every new question requires a development cycle




BI Teams


Skilled analysts writing SQL and building reports
Bottleneck: 3-week turnaround for cross-system questions
High value, but limited bandwidth




AI Agents


Any question, any time, in plain English
Cross-system queries with automatic reconciliation
Not a replacement — a force multiplier




Walk through each card. Say: “Dashboards are great for monitoring known metrics. But they cannot answer questions they were not designed for. Every new question requires a development cycle.”
“BI teams are highly skilled. But they are a bottleneck. The 3-week email chain is not because anyone is bad at their job — it is because there are too many questions and too few analysts.”
“AI agents sit in a different category. Any question, any time, in plain English. But I want to be clear: this is not a replacement for dashboards or BI teams. It is a force multiplier. Dashboards still monitor KPIs. BI teams still build validated data models. The agent handles the ad hoc questions that today go unanswered or take weeks.”
Transition: “Now it is your turn. Let me get you logged into the Meridian app.”

title: Session 3: AI Meets Your Data
text:
Hands-On: Your First Enterprise Queries

This opens the hands-on segment (minutes 33-53). This is the longest segment of the session — 20 minutes. Students will log into the Meridian app and work through guided prompts, progressing from single-system to cross-system queries. Make sure login credentials are ready to share.

title: Session 3: AI Meets Your Data
text:
Getting Started


Log In Now


Open your browser and navigate to the Meridian app URL (in chat)
Log in with your provided credentials
You should see a chat interface — type your first question



Start with single-system queries to get comfortable. Then we will push into cross-system territory.


Share the URL and credentials via Zoom chat. Give students 2 minutes to log in. Troubleshoot any access issues. Once most people are in, say: “Great. You should see a chat interface. This is the same tool I just demoed. You are going to work through a series of guided queries, starting simple and getting more complex.”
“Start with single-system queries to get comfortable. These hit one database at a time. Then we will push into cross-system territory, where the agent has to query multiple systems and merge results.”

title: Session 3: AI Meets Your Data
text:
Round 1: Single-System Queries



Salesforce (Industrial CRM)


“What is the total pipeline value for the Industrial division?”
“Who are the top 5 customers by revenue in 2025?”




NetSuite (Finance)


“What was total revenue by quarter for 2025?”
“Show operating expenses by category for Q4”




Workday (HR)


“How many employees does each division have?”
“What is the average tenure by department?”




Say: “Round 1 — single-system queries. Each of these hits one database. Try at least two from different systems. I have suggested some prompts, but feel free to ask your own questions.”
Give students about 5-6 minutes on this round. Circulate (watch Zoom chat for questions). Point out interesting results if you see them. The goal is to build comfort with the interface before moving to cross-system queries.
After 5-6 minutes: “Good. Now let us make it harder.”

title: Session 3: AI Meets Your Data
text:
Round 2: Cross-System Queries



Try These


“Which customers buy from multiple divisions?”
“What is revenue per employee by division?”
“Compare support ticket volume to revenue by division”
“Which division’s headcount grew fastest relative to revenue?”




Watch For


The agent querying multiple systems sequentially
Python merge steps appearing in the response
Fuzzy name matching across CRMs
Narrative that synthesizes data from different sources




Say: “Round 2 — cross-system queries. These require the agent to pull from multiple databases and merge the results. Watch what happens behind the scenes. You will see it query one system, then another, then use Python to combine the data.”
“Try the suggested prompts, or ask your own cross-system questions. The more specific your prompt, the better the output.”
Give students about 7-8 minutes. This is where the real “aha” moments happen.

“This requires data from at least two systems — CRM for customer revenue concentration, and Zendesk for support tickets. Ask the Meridian app and see what you get.”

title: Session 3: AI Meets Your Data
text:
Workshop: Your Company’s Data Map

This opens the data map workshop (minutes 53-63). Students shift from the Meridian simulation to thinking about their own companies. This work carries forward through the rest of the course and into the capstone.

title: Session 3: AI Meets Your Data
text:
Map Your Systems


Individual Exercise (10 minutes)


List your company’s 3-5 core systems (CRM, ERP, HR, finance, support, etc.)
For each system: What data lives there? Who owns it? How do you access it today?
Identify one cross-system question nobody can answer easily today
Estimate: Who answers it now? How long does it take?



This data map is the foundation for your capstone strategy. Be specific — system names, not categories.


Say: “Now I want you to think about your own company. Take 10 minutes and map your data landscape. List your 3 to 5 core systems — not categories like ‘CRM,’ but actual system names. Salesforce. SAP. Workday. Whatever your company uses.”
“For each system, note what data lives there and who owns it. Then identify one cross-system question that nobody can answer easily today. The kind of question that, like the CEO’s question about multi-division customers, sounds simple but requires pulling data from multiple systems.”
“This data map carries forward. It becomes the foundation for your capstone strategy in Session 8. Be specific.”
Give students the full 10 minutes. Circulate and help anyone who is stuck.

title: Session 3: AI Meets Your Data
section: Breakout: How Scattered Is Your Data?
text:
Breakout: How Scattered Is Your Data?

This opens the breakout discussion (minutes 63-78). Groups of 3-4 compare their data maps and look for patterns. Assign breakout rooms now.

title: Session 3: AI Meets Your Data
text:
Breakout Discussion (15 minutes)



Compare Data Maps


What systems do you have in common?
Where is integration hardest?
What cross-system questions would have the most business value?




Report Back


One common pattern your group identified
One surprising idiosyncrasy unique to someone’s company
What would the “3-week email chain” look like at your organizations?




Say: “Breakout time. Groups of 3 to 4. Compare your data maps. I want you to find what you have in common — most of you will share some of the same systems — and where you diverge. What cross-system questions would be most valuable at your companies?”
“When we come back, each group reports: one common pattern and one surprising idiosyncrasy. You have 15 minutes.”
Send students to breakout rooms. After 12 minutes, broadcast a 3-minute warning. When groups return, take 3-4 minutes for report-backs. Common patterns often include: everyone has a CRM-to-finance gap; HR data is always siloed; customer naming is inconsistent everywhere.

title: Session 3: AI Meets Your Data
text:
What We’re Skipping

This opens the expectations calibration segment (minutes 78-87). You are about to name everything the demo did NOT show — the pre-work required before any agent can query real systems. This is critical for preventing the “we can do this on Monday” reaction.

title: Session 3: AI Meets Your Data
section: The 60-90 Days the Demo Didn’t Show
text:
The 60-90 Days the Demo Didn’t Show



Data Cataloging


Inventory every table, column, and relationship
Document what each field actually means
Map data quality issues




Schema Documentation


Write descriptions the LLM can understand
The system prompt is “institutional knowledge”
Gets it wrong? Fix the schema description




API Access


Provision read-only database connections
Set up authentication and rate limits
Security review for each system




PII Classification


Identify sensitive fields (salary, SSN, health data)
Define access control rules per role
Decide what the LLM is allowed to see




Say: “Before we wrap, I need to calibrate expectations. Everything you saw today was impressive. But the Meridian demo hides something important: the 60 to 90 days of pre-work that happened before the first question was ever asked.”
Walk through each card: “Someone had to catalog every table and column. Someone had to write schema descriptions the LLM can understand — that system prompt is not automatic, it is carefully authored institutional knowledge. Someone had to provision API access to each system with proper security. And someone had to classify every field for PII sensitivity and define access control rules.”
“This pre-work is real. It is not glamorous. And it is the difference between a demo and a production deployment. I name it now so that when you go back to your organizations and say ‘I saw something amazing,’ you also say ‘and here is what it takes to get there.’”

title: Session 3: AI Meets Your Data
text:
How Data Moves Today



The Traditional Answer: Data Warehouse


Move all data into one place (Snowflake, BigQuery, Redshift)
Cost: $500K–$5M+ to build
Timeline: 6–18 months
Advantage: one source of truth, fast queries
Downside: stale data, expensive, slow to change




The Alternative: Query Where It Lives


AI agent queries each system via API
No data movement — always current
Cost: lower upfront, per-query costs
Advantage: fresh data, fast to deploy
Downside: slower queries, more complex orchestration




Most enterprises will use both — a warehouse for core reporting, agents for ad hoc cross-system questions.


Say: “There are two approaches to getting cross-system answers. The traditional answer is a data warehouse — move everything into one place. This is proven technology. It works. But it costs $500K to $5 million, takes 6 to 18 months, and the data is always at least slightly stale.”
“The alternative is what you just saw: query each system where it lives. No data movement. Always current. Faster to deploy. But the queries are slower and the orchestration is more complex.”
“In practice, most enterprises will use both. A warehouse for core reporting and dashboards. Agents for ad hoc cross-system questions that the warehouse was not designed to answer.”
“We will dig deeper into deployment architecture in Session 5. For now, just know that both options exist and there are real tradeoffs.”

title: Session 3: AI Meets Your Data
text:
Homework

Wrap-up segment (minutes 87-90). Assign homework and preview Session 4.

Bring your best and worst Meridian answers to Session 4



Next session: We go from asking questions to producing complete deliverables — memos, reports, risk assessments. The agent becomes your reporting pipeline.


Say: “Homework. First, log into the Meridian app this week and ask at least 10 business questions. Push it. Ask at least 3 cross-system questions. Write down your best answer and your worst answer — we will use both in Session 4.”
“Second, complete your data map. List at least 5 systems your company uses and the top 3 cross-system questions you cannot answer today. This carries forward to your capstone.”
“Next session, we go from asking questions to producing complete deliverables. Instead of getting a number or a chart, you will get a board-ready memo. See you Wednesday.”

title: Session 5: What Does Deployment Look Like?
text:
From Prototype to Production

“Morgan Stanley deployed GPT-4 to 16,000 financial advisors — choosing a cloud API with zero data retention to satisfy SEC and FINRA requirements. The bottleneck wasn’t the technology. It was the architecture decision.”


— Morgan Stanley case study (provided as pre-reading)


Welcome back everyone. This is Session 5 and today we are shifting gears from using AI agents to deploying them. Let this quote from the Morgan Stanley case sit with you for a moment. Sixteen thousand financial advisors, SEC and FINRA breathing down their neck, and the hardest part was not getting the model to work. It was deciding where the model would run and what data it would be allowed to see. That is exactly what today is about.
You arrived having read three things: the Morgan Stanley case study, the AIMultiple article on cloud versus local LLMs, and the data security primer. So today is not a lecture day. Today is a discussion day. I am going to push you to apply what you read to your own companies, your own regulatory environments, your own data. By the end of the session, you will have a written deployment profile for your capstone use case.
[TIMING: Spend about 2 minutes on this opening slide. Let the quote land, then transition to objectives.]

title: Session 5: What Does Deployment Look Like?
text:
Today’s Learning Objectives


By the end of this session, you will be able to:


Compare LLM deployment options: cloud API, on-premise, and hybrid
Identify data security risks and match them to mitigation strategies
Evaluate build vs. buy vs. extend using a structured framework
Draft a deployment profile for your capstone use case



Let me orient you to where we are headed in the next 85 minutes. Four objectives, all of them practical. By the time you leave today, you will have compared deployment options, matched security approaches to your industry, worked through a build-versus-buy framework, and drafted an actual deployment profile that becomes a section of your capstone.
Notice the arc: the first three objectives give you the frameworks. The fourth objective is where you apply them. Everything we do in the first hour is in service of that deployment profile at the end.
[TIMING: 1 minute. Move quickly to the homework debrief.]

title: Session 5: What Does Deployment Look Like?
text:
Homework Debrief

Alright, let us start with where we left off. You were asked to produce a complete deliverable using the Meridian app — a memo, a comparison, a risk assessment. I want to hear what you built and, more importantly, whether you would trust it enough to send it upstairs.
[TIMING: This section divider is a brief transition. Move immediately to the next slide.]

title: Session 5: What Does Deployment Look Like?
text:
What Did You Build?



Share Your Deliverable


Walk us through the deliverable you produced in the Meridian app
What question did it answer?
Who is the audience — and what format did you choose?




The Board-Ready Test


What looks right at first glance?
What would you verify before sending to your board?
Where could the agent have introduced errors?




The gap between “interesting demo” and “decision-grade output” is exactly what today’s session is about.


I would like three or four volunteers to walk us through what they produced. Keep it to about a minute each. Tell us: what question did you ask, who was the intended audience, and what format did you choose — a memo, a dashboard summary, a comparison table?
After each share, I want the rest of the room to apply the board-ready test. Look at it with fresh eyes. What looks polished and credible at first glance? And then the harder question: what would you want to verify before you put your name on it and sent it to your board of directors? Where might the agent have introduced errors — wrong date ranges, double-counted customers, averages that include data they should not?
This is the perfect bridge into today’s topic. The gap between an interesting demo output and something you would stake your reputation on is exactly the gap that deployment decisions, data security, and governance are designed to close. That gap is what the rest of this session addresses.
[TIMING: 8 minutes total for this segment. Call on 3-4 students, about 1.5 minutes each, with brief reactions from the room. If participation is slow, share your own example from the Meridian app to get things started. Transition: “Those verification instincts you just described? They are exactly the right instincts. Now let us talk about the Morgan Stanley case, because they faced the same questions at massive scale.”]

title: Session 5: What Does Deployment Look Like?
text:
Two Separate Decisions

Before we get into the Morgan Stanley discussion, I want to plant one important idea that connects back to Session 4.
[TIMING: Brief transition slide. Move to the next slide immediately.]

title: Session 5: What Does Deployment Look Like?
text:
Where Does It Run?



Where does YOUR CODE run?


Your application, database connectors, orchestration logic
Typically: your cloud tenant, your VPC, your servers
You control this entirely




Where does THE LLM run?


The large language model that interprets questions and generates SQL
Cloud API, on-premise, or hybrid
This is the deployment decision




Bridge from Session 4: the agent architecture has two halves. Your code and the LLM are separate deployment decisions — and the security implications are different for each.


In Session 4 we asked: where does the code run? We talked about where the code executes — the orchestration logic, the database connectors, the application layer. That code runs on your servers. You control it entirely. But there is a second piece: the large language model itself, the thing that interprets the natural language question and generates SQL. Where does that run? That is a completely separate decision, and it is the deployment decision we are focused on today.
Here is why this distinction matters. You can run your application code on your own servers, inside your own VPC, fully locked down — and still be sending your query results to OpenAI’s servers for interpretation. Or you can keep everything local, but then you are running a smaller, less capable model. These are different tradeoffs with different security implications. The Morgan Stanley case you read is a perfect illustration of how a real company navigated this exact decision.
Let me turn it over to the room. You read the Morgan Stanley case. What struck you?
[TIMING: About 5 minutes for this slide. Spend 2 minutes on the bridge explanation, then open it up to the Morgan Stanley case discussion which continues for the next 7-8 minutes. Key discussion questions: Was cloud API with zero data retention the right call for Morgan Stanley? What would your company have done differently? The a16z survey says 90% of enterprises are testing third-party AI — does that match your experience?]
[DISCUSSION FACILITATION: If the room is quiet, start with a direct question: “Who here works in an industry with regulatory constraints similar to Morgan Stanley’s? What would your compliance team have said about sending data to a cloud API?” Let 3-4 people respond. Push back gently: “But Morgan Stanley did use the cloud API — they just negotiated zero data retention. Is that enough for your industry?”]

title: Session 5: What Does Deployment Look Like?
text:
Three Deployment Options



Cloud API


OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, etc.
Pay per query (~$0.01–0.05)
No infrastructure to manage
Data leaves your network
Best for: fast start, variable workloads




On-Premise / Private Cloud


Self-hosted open-source models
$50–200K+ upfront investment
Full data control
Requires ML engineering staff
Best for: regulated industries, sensitive data




Hybrid


Route by sensitivity level
Sensitive queries stay on-prem
General queries use cloud API
More complex orchestration
Best for: balanced risk/cost




You read about these three options in the AIMultiple article, so I am not going to lecture through them. Instead, let me add the context the article did not cover and then I want your reactions.
Cloud API is where almost everyone starts. You saw the a16z number: 90% of enterprises are testing third-party AI tools. The economics are compelling — pennies per query, no infrastructure, no ML engineers on staff. The catch is that your data leaves your network. For some of you, that is a non-starter. For others, with the right contractual protections, it is perfectly acceptable.
On-premise is the other end of the spectrum. You are running open-source models like Llama or Mistral on your own GPUs. Full data control, nothing leaves your building. But you are looking at fifty to two hundred thousand dollars in GPU infrastructure, plus you need people who know how to run these models. The quality gap between open-source models and the frontier cloud models is shrinking, but it is still real.
Hybrid is where most sophisticated enterprises are heading. You route queries by sensitivity level. Someone asks about public revenue data? Send it to the cloud API, get the best model. Someone asks about individual compensation or pre-announcement financials? Route that to the on-premise model. The orchestration is more complex, but you get the best of both worlds.
Quick show of hands: based on what you read and what you know about your company, which of these three feels like the right starting point for you? Cloud API? On-premise? Hybrid?
[TIMING: 4 minutes. Use the show of hands to gauge where the room is and transition to the cost slide. If most people say cloud API, note that and say “Let us look at the economics of that choice.”]

title: Session 5: What Does Deployment Look Like?
text:
Cost Comparison



$0.01–0.05


Per simple query — Cloud API (multi-step queries cost more)




$50–200K+


Upfront — On-premise GPU infrastructure




90%


Of enterprises testing third-party AI tools (a16z 2025 CIO survey)




Cloud API dominates early adoption. But as query volume scales and data sensitivity increases, the economics shift. Morgan Stanley’s choice: cloud API with zero data retention — the LLM never stores the conversation.


Let me put some numbers on this. A simple cloud API query costs one to five cents. That sounds trivial, but do the math: if you have a thousand users running twenty queries a day, that is two hundred to a thousand dollars a day just in API costs. And multi-step queries — the kind we have been running in the Meridian app, where the agent queries multiple systems and merges results — those can cost ten to fifty cents each because they involve multiple LLM calls.
On-premise, you are paying fifty to two hundred thousand dollars upfront for GPU infrastructure, plus ongoing costs for electricity, cooling, and the engineering staff to maintain it. But after that, your marginal cost per query approaches zero.
The crossover point depends on volume and sensitivity. For a pilot with a hundred users, cloud API wins every time. For an enterprise-wide deployment with sensitive data, the math changes. Morgan Stanley made an interesting choice: they went with cloud API but negotiated zero data retention. The LLM processes the query and immediately forgets it. No conversation history is stored on the vendor’s servers. That was how they satisfied SEC and FINRA requirements while still getting access to the best model.
[TIMING: 3 minutes. Transition: “Now let us see the data security problem in action. I want to show you something that should make you uncomfortable.”]

title: Session 5: What Does Deployment Look Like?
text:
The Data Security Problem

We have been talking about deployment options in the abstract. Now I want to make the data security problem concrete. You read about the four approaches in the primer. Let me show you why they matter.
[TIMING: Brief transition. Move immediately to the demo slide.]

title: Session 5: What Does Deployment Look Like?
text:
Demo: Data Security in Action



Version 1: No Guardrails


“Show me individual salary data for the Engineering team”
Agent returns names, titles, and exact salaries
Technically correct — but a governance disaster




Version 2: Guardrailed


Same question — blocked or returns aggregates only
“Average salary for Engineering: $142K (n=47)”
The LLM sees the same data; the guardrails control what it returns




The question isn’t whether the AI can access the data. It’s whether it should — and who decides.


[DEMO INSTRUCTIONS: Open the Meridian app. First, with guardrails disabled or in the ungoverned version, type: “Show me individual salary data for the Engineering team.” Let the agent return names, titles, and exact salaries. Let the room see it. Pause for a beat. Then switch to the guardrailed version and ask the same question. Show that it either blocks the query entirely or returns only aggregates: “Average salary for Engineering: $142K, n=47.”]
Look at that first result. The agent did exactly what you asked. The SQL was correct. The data is accurate. And if anyone in your company could run this query, you have a governance disaster on your hands. Individual salary data, by name, returned in seconds to anyone with access to the chat interface.
Now look at the second version. Same question, same underlying data, but the guardrails intercept the query and return only the aggregate. The LLM has the same access to the data — the difference is what the system allows it to return.
This is the core insight from your pre-reading: the question is not whether the AI can access the data. It is whether it should, and who makes that decision. Let me ask the room: which of the four data security approaches from the primer would have prevented that first result?
[TIMING: 8 minutes total for this demo section. Spend 3-4 minutes on the live demo, then 4-5 minutes on the discussion that follows. If the demo has technical issues, describe the scenario verbally using the slide content — the point lands either way.]
[DISCUSSION PROMPT: “Which of the four approaches from the primer — zero data retention, data masking, on-premise models, or role-based filtering — would have prevented that first result?” Answer: role-based query filtering is the direct answer, but zero data retention alone would not have helped here because the problem is not where the data goes, it is who can ask for it.]
